speaker: interviewer
text: Ernest Ari Katz, Aaron Katz. interviewer Aaron Weiss, Northbrook, Illinois, USA. English. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned just now that your mother had a sister and three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I forgot-- I failed to mention it at the time when you asked me who lived in our household. There was my aunt that lived in Slovakia. And somehow they took away the Jews from Slovakia. But again, it did not register with us, because we didn't know why they were taking them, and where they took them away. But her husband was in-- had an important position in business, and they needed to keep him there. So they did not take him away till much later. And we were under the impression that they are taking him in-- in a labor camp. And as a matter of fact, I remember my aunt was worried that he bought himself some good shoes, that to make sure not to forget those shoes to take along with him. And she was smuggled over the-- through the border. Let's see, that must have been in 19-- 1943. And she stayed with-- with her parents and us. She was younger than my mother. She had three little girls. And she was taken away with everybody else. And she was killed. I know-- I know my grandfather, my grandmother, my mother, my aunt, and two little girls, they were all taken on one transport. They were killed the same day. I know not far from our place, which was called Seredna, I had an aunt and an uncle with eight children. None of those survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother died when he was 17 years old. He died at home from an illness that was misdiagnosed. And I remember my mother went through torture getting over that situation. It was very, very bad. Very touchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's come to the United States now. So you left-- you came here from Paris, and then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Paris. I came to New York with my two sisters. My father didn't have much. He wasn't feeling too well. But he had a sister living in Brooklyn, and we were staying with them. A couple of days later, the Schneiders arrived, which is Etta and her two sisters and brother. I came with them from New York to Chicago. And-- --we were not getting married. We didn't want to get married in Europe. I don't know whether my wife wanted to get married before, but it was my decision out of respect to my father, because I wanted him to be at my wedding. And we knew that we are going to get married. I came to Chicago. That was in 1946, April or May. We got married in the same year, 1946-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: November, right? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --it was a wedding where we had our family together. My father came from New York. And we established a homestead in Chicago. Got an apartment-- was very hard to come by. We lived in a rooming house. It wasn't too pleasant. The washroom was on the outside. The cockroaches were in the inside. And until we were able to find a better apartment, I had a job working at a grocery store. This is during the summertime. I remember my hands were frozen because I was working with produce that was delivered in ice. And I didn't work [INAUDIBLE] too long at that job, because my uncle decided that that wasn't for me. I think I was making about $26 or $28 a week, but luckily my wife was a seamstress and she was making in the 90s, so that money we saved up. And naturally, we had to save up the money, because when we came out to this country, the tickets were $400 some odd, the airplane tickets. In 1946, $400 was a little fortune. So for myself and Etta we had [INAUDIBLE] came minus $900, and we had to save up that money and pay back our relatives that put out that money. Then I was working by [? Hillman's. ?] I was working by [? Galbraith's. ?] And finally I wound up with the Vienna Sausage Company in the meat business. I started out as a-- working in a store, and I became a store manager. Then I worked myself up to the situation where I became the national meat buyer. And things were going along pretty well. We had little babies. And they grew up-- Marshall, Jerry, and Stephen. And-- --we were living a nice life, a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were happy to be alive. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Americans and American Jews when you first came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treated OK, except they didn't want to hear about our plights, what happened to Europe. They kept on saying, forget about it. Forget about it. Don't talk about it anymore. It was gone. You have to-- you have to forget about it, which fortunately, we didn't forget. Some of the things were wiped out in our mind subconsciously. But it's good to remember. It makes you feel good. It's a horrible thing to go through. But you have to remember. We were treated a little bit like lost relatives. I remember at our wedding, it was planned-- it was planned for November. And the way everybody was invited, we attended some friend's bar mitzvah party-- where my uncle was living, his landlord's son's bar mitzvah party. And he made an announcement that we are going to get married, and everybody who was at that party is invited to our wedding. And they came to the wedding, but they figured we didn't have anything, so we didn't need anything. Nobody gave us a present-- nobody. We didn't get any wedding presents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wanted to tell the story only for one reason-- that it should be preserved for the future. We might have a few more good years left in ourselves. And if we don't tell the story now, we will not have a chance to tell it later. And I think it's very important for everybody to tell their story and to make sure that they remember, and they know about it, and history is going to record it as such that it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have your experiences had any effect in the way that you have raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they must have. Etta did a very good job at raising the kids. They are very responsible. They went the right way. Their upbringing was such that she instilled in them that they can do anything that they set out to do. And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] the proof of the pudding is the end results, that it's here. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And now, thank God, we have an extended family, and we enjoy them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does your present level of belief and observance of Judaism compare with pre-war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pre-war, I was brought up in a very Orthodox religious family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wouldn't commit any kind of a sin, because I was afraid that God is going to strike me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know he doesn't do those things anymore. Now I know that he doesn't do those things for any sins-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --because I know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --my grandfather was a very righteous man. So was my grandmother. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So was my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took them away from the sick bed and killed them in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I cannot picture my little cousins, and my mother, and grandmother, and grandfather undressing before they took them into the gas chambers. [CRYING] I know that [SIGHS] I was in Washington to see the Holocaust Memorial. We went through the memorial. Etta and I, we couldn't see-- we couldn't spend too much time in there, because it was hurting badly. The only thing that I remember, that's stuck in my memory from the museum-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --was the mass of people, what they looked like after they were gassed, when they opened the doors. There is a-- there is a part of it. I think it's made with white clay. And this is how I picture my mother. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- it's unimaginable. You just cannot understand it. I mean, what these people went through, what went through their mind? They knew that it's their end, I'm sure. How do you cope?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I don't know. [CRYING] It's tough. You try to-- in order to achieve some kind of normalcy, you're trying to lock it of your memory. But it comes back. It's haunting you-- nightmares. Your children and your grandchildren are taken into the camps, and you see that in front of your eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Will power. Nothing but will power and hope. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know when [INAUDIBLE] when I was liberated, I weighed about 65 or 70 pounds. My skin-- the dirt was embedded in my skin so deeply that it took weeks-- it took weeks for it to clear out. I mean, it was just black. It was just nothing. And I remember by the liberation, the sweater that I had, had multicolored lice-- multicolor. The lice had lice. It was unreal-- big ones, small ones, all kinds. It was a great torture, and I'm very happy that we survived. And I couldn't expect any better from life that life has dealt. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Occasionally, [SIGHS] we go to high schools. As a matter of fact, we went into the schools of our grandchildren. As a matter of fact, we are scheduled for May to go to [? Alana's ?] school. There are going to be about 95 kids. Etta and I are going to tell them about our story. But a couple of years ago, we went to the same school with Michelle, with Michelle's classmates. and we had beautiful letters telling us how much they appreciate us telling them the story and how brave we were to talk about it, and so forth and so on. And hopefully it's going to help for history, that it's not going to be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message you'd like to give your children or your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just continue life. Be upright. Do what you are doing best. And they are doing a wonderful job, all my children. All my grandchildren are carrying on the tradition-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] --the way Etta and I would have hoped that they would. We are very proud of them, and they're doing a beautiful job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Katz, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, and all the people who view this, thank you very much for sharing with us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your life. In this photograph, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sweet mother. She was 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rivka Katz, known by the name Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date the photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date the photo was taken, I would say around 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place the photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it from my father, who lived in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother again, a couple of years later, I think, 1922. At that time, I think she was married already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place the photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place the photo was taken was Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my father that was in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture is of, from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my brother, Ben Zion, who was 5 years old in this picture. In the middle was my sister Magda, who was 1 year old, and myself, who was 6 years old-- 7 years old. Sorry about that. And this picture was taken in Munkacs to send to my father, who was in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- the photo was taken in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there any significance to what you're wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm wearing a sailor outfit. Everybody else did that in those days, [LAUGHS] those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken a year later, in 1930. It's of my brother, Ben Zion. I am next to him, standing, with a grin on my face. And my youngest--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back. My youngest sister, Esther, who was 1 year old in this picture, and the older sister-- her older sister, Magda, was 2 years old in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your youngest sister was in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This picture-- this picture was taken in Munkacs, again, for the purpose of sending it to my father in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture Is of my brother, Ben Zion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 12 years old. He is on the right. He is standing on the right side of me. And I'm standing erect next to him. I was 14. I think the picture was taken in 1937 by a friend of my father's, who was visiting from the United States at the time to take along to my father. And I found it in my father's possession after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, does he have payos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he has beautiful payos. I mean, he's standing sideways and the payos are showing up beautifully. Those are long payos curled up to be small.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a school report card, a Czech school. I was 10 years old. I think it was 1933. And if you look closely, you can see all A's. Only one B, and that is probably from-- the B is for what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Gym class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gym class, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also a report card. The last one that we saw was from the Czech grammar school. This is from a Russian high school. All the marks are written out, handwritten, so I can tell you they're all excellent marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, cut. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of myself, on the right side, and smoking a cigarette. At that time I just started smoking, getting used to life. And my brother-in-law, Joe, on the left side of me, we were walking down in Prague trying to find our way to Zatec to establish our home, to bring our sisters and the rest of the family together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was 1946, right after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I-- we might probably found it-- no, this was after the war already. I got a picture. I paid for it in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture? Picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made in 1951. That's my father's wife, Jenny--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side. Next to him is my father. Next to my father is my sister's mother-in-law, my sister Magda, and her father-in-law on the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her father-in-law and mother-in-law's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is [? Sibulsky. ?] Mr. And Mrs. Nathan [? Sibulsky. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was made here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at the same time, in 1951. It shows from the right my sister Esther, next to her my sister Magda, my lovely wife, Etta, my father, [INAUDIBLE], and his wife, Jenny. You don't know what I said. This beautiful couple is myself with my lovely wife, Etta, who are responsible for all the good things that have happened in our lives. And the next tape, I'll be happy to dedicate to life, to show my children and grandchildren, which we are going to show soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Katz, why don't you introduce this big mishpocha here?

speaker: interviewee
text: On behalf of my lovely wife, Etta, and myself, we would like to dedicate this next segment to life. Like I said, to life, to life, l'chaim. I would like to introduce my eldest son, Marshall, and his lovely wife, Tobey, [? Stuart, ?] Alex, and Rachel. Next we have our daughter, Geri, her husband, Hal, and Geri's children, Adam, who is not here, he's in Michigan, Michelle, [? Alanna, ?] Hal's children Michael, who is not here, Scott, who is not here, and we are very happy to have Tommy here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Tommy?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Tommy. And then have [NON-ENGLISH]. I interpret that as being the last one is as much loved as the first one, although that's not the proper [LAUGHS] interpretation, Steven and his lovely wife, [? Rochelle. ?] We have [? Yoni, ?] we have Ariel, and Isaac. This is our whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a beautiful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one more to come. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right, one more to come, [? Rochelle. ?] And would the children like to say anything? We'll start with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. First of all, I want to congratulate my father for remembering everybody's name. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: See if you can get him for birthdays. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And I just also want my parents know how proud I am of them, that they were able to do this today. I think it's a very important thing they did. And I don't have the benefit of having heard what they said yet, but just for posterity, I want everyone to realize that there is a lot more to their lives than what went on this tape. And even though there was a lot of hardship involved in the early part of their lives, they were still able to raise a normal, loving family, and lead normal lives. And that's quite a testament to both of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. That's very true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We enjoyed every minute of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Geri?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm really happy that this is being done, because all my life, when I heard about their stories growing up, which we were always told, unlike a lot of our cousins that didn't know what had happened, I used to think as a little girl, how come nobody knows? And even when I'd go to school, in the history books, there would be like one line about a concentration camp. And the teachers didn't even know. And I'd say, I don't know, why do I have to know? And it was very difficult growing up knowing it and not being able to even share it with anybody. So this is a very important thing that my parents are doing. And I'm very proud of them for doing it. And I'm also very happy that the rest of the world gets to know at least a little bit, because they can't know everything, and everything that we felt and felt for our parents. And I'm just very happy that something is being done, finally, and people will remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marshall?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I want to tell my parents how much I love both of them, and how courageous I think they are to have gone through with this project, and how they were able, in spite of having to go through much hardship at a very young age, how they were able to raise a family with love and a sense of spirit and a sense of living life to the fullest. And what you see here in the picture is the fruits of their labor. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Love you all. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]
